coredata.dc:title: On the fate of the Ti catalyst during hydrogen cycling of sodium alanate
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Haiduc, A.G.
Stil, H.A.
Schwarz, M.A.
Paulus, P.
Geerlings, J.J.C.
coredata.dc:description: The state of the titanium catalyst species formed upon doping and hydrogen cycling of NaAlH4 with Ti-catalysts was studied by X-ray diffraction analysis. After doping with TiCl3, Ti is present as an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution, while for the Ti(OBu)4 doped system, Ti exists as an XRD-amorphous phase. Cycling of hydrogen for 1.5 times results in transformation of these initially different Ti compounds into similar compounds, whose composition is dependent on the temperature. Thus, if low temperatures were used during the cycling process (up to 175°C) an amorphous Al–Ti alloy formed, while upon using high temperatures (200°C and higher) Ti was present as an Al x Ti intermetallic. Correlation of the XRD and hydrogen desorption curves shows that the most active catalyst species in the present system is the amorphous Al–Ti alloy, whereas a decreased catalytic activity is found for the Al x Ti intermetallic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage
Alanate
Metal hydrides
X-ray diffraction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Powder XRD pattern of undoped and TiCl3-doped NaAlH4. Positions where main TiCl3reflections should appear are indicated by arrows.

Observed (markers), calculated (solid line), and difference profiles for Rietveld refinement of powder XRD data for a NaAlH4 sample doped with 10mol% TiCl3. Si was added as an internal standard for calibration of the horizontal axis.
Powder XRD pattern of a 10mol% TiCl3 doped NaAlH4 sample, showing development of a broad reflection at the high angle side of the main Al peak, centred around 40° 2θ and ending sharply at 41° 2θ.
Powder XRD pattern from NaAlH4 doped with 12mol% Ti(OBu)4.
Powder XRD pattern of a 10mol% TiCl3 doped NaH sample showing the formation of TiH2 upon doping.
Comparison of XRD powder patterns of the precipitate from THF separation; (—) as precipitated at RT and (†) after heating up at 475°C. The phase labelling scheme is given in the inset.
Comparison of powder XRD patterns of NaAlH4 doped with 10mol% TiCl3 at various stages of desorption.
Comparison of powder XRD patterns of NaAlH4 doped with 12mol% Ti(OBu)4 undesorbed vs. fully desorbed at 150°C, 1.5 cycles.
Comparison of powder patterns of 10mol.% TiCl3 doped and undoped NaAlH4. Note change in the shape of the Al peak (38.5–2θ) in the doped material upon dehydrogenation.

Powder XRD pattern close-up of the main Al peak showing changes occurring upon desorption of hydrogen. Thermal treatment at elevated temperatures (475°C) results in formation of an Al–Ti intermetallic compound.
Rate curve for the first desorption half cycle of NaAlH4 doped with 10mol% TiCl3.
Rate curves for second desorption half cycle of NaAlH4 doped with 10mol% TiCl3.
Hydrogen absorption curves for 10mol% TiCl3 doped NaAlH4 previously desorbed at various temperatures.
Powder XRD pattern showing the progressive disappearance of the low broad reflection with increasing temperature.

Powder XRD pattern presenting the changes in main Al reflection at 38.5° 2θ at various temperatures showing an asymmetrical broadening and the development of a shoulder towards higher angles with increasing temperature.

Powder XRD pattern presenting the changes in Al reflection at 44.7° 2θ at various temperatures showing an asymmetrical broadening and the development of a shoulder in the Al peak towards higher angles with increasing temperature.
Hydrogen desorption curves for NaAlH4 doped with 12mol% Ti(OBu)4.
Hydrogen absorption curve for NaAlH4 doped with 12mol% Ti(OBu)4.

article.ce:title: On the fate of the Ti catalyst during hydrogen cycling of sodium alanate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shell Global Solutions International B.V., Shell Research and Technology Centre Amsterdam, Badhuisweg 3, P.O. Box 38000, 1030 BN Amsterdam, The Netherlands

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The state of the titanium catalyst species formed upon doping and hydrogen cycling of NaAlH4 with Ti-catalysts was studied by X-ray diffraction analysis. After doping with TiCl3, Ti is present as an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution, while for the Ti(OBu)4 doped system, Ti exists as an XRD-amorphous phase. Cycling of hydrogen for 1.5 times results in transformation of these initially different Ti compounds into similar compounds, whose composition is dependent on the temperature. Thus, if low temperatures were used during the cycling process (up to 175°C) an amorphous Al–Ti alloy formed, while upon using high temperatures (200°C and higher) Ti was present as an Al x Ti intermetallic. Correlation of the XRD and hydrogen desorption curves shows that the most active catalyst species in the present system is the amorphous Al–Ti alloy, whereas a decreased catalytic activity is found for the Al x Ti intermetallic.

ce:section.ce:para:
The absence of efficient storage technology is the biggest technical impediment for the introduction of direct hydrogen fuel. Various hydrogen storage options can be envisaged and are being investigated, but they all suffer from fundamental or technical weaknesses or from lack of societal acceptance. The challenge for the scientific community is to design alternative light weight hydrogen storage materials that meet the criteria for transport applications. The complex alanates have great potential as hydrogen storage materials due to their inherently high theoretical hydrogen capacities. Sodium alanate (NaAlH4) was until recently considered to release hydrogen irreversibly. The breakthrough work by Bogdanovic and Schwikardi [1] showed that it could be made reversible under moderate conditions by doping with transition metals, particularly Ti compounds. The release of hydrogen occurs in consecutive steps, as depicted in reactions (1) and (2):
(1)
NaAl
H 4
↔
1 3
N
a 3
Al
H 6
+
2 3
Al +
H 2
(2)
1 3
N
a 3
Al
H 6
+
2 3
Al +
H 2
↔ NaH + Al +
3 2
H 2

The first step releases 3.7wt.% hydrogen and the second 1.9wt.%, with a total of 5.6wt.% for the overall reaction. The temperatures for atmospheric pressure desorption are about 33°C for the first step (NaAlH4 decomposition) and 110°C for the second step (Na3AlH6 decomposition). The release of hydrogen from NaH occurs at temperatures above 300°C, which makes it impractical for most technical applications. In summary catalysed NaAlH4 shows a great promise for meeting the requirements for on-board hydrogen storage. One major disadvantage of NaAlH4 is the slow hydriding and dehydriding kinetics below about 150°C. Various compounds have been tested [2–4] but Ti has proved to be the most effective.

Up till now there is only very limited fundamental understanding on why the hydrogen cycling kinetics is enhanced upon doping the hydride. Although much work has been published on Ti-doped sodium alanate, there is still no clear insight regarding the exact state of the Ti-catalyst upon doping and hydrogen cycling. This is due to the fact that the catalyst is almost invisible to most analytical techniques at all stages of the hydrogen cycling process. Contradictory results have been reported [3,5–7]. Some studies claim that the catalyst species are located on the surface of the particles as pure metals or alloys. Formation of an alloy of Al and the metal dopant has been reported to occur upon ball milling or during hydrogen cycling. TiAl3 formation was observed upon reacting stoichiometric amounts of LiAlH4 [8] or NaAlH4 [9] with TiCl4 or TiCl3, respectively. Upon doping NaAlH4 with both Fe(OEt)3 and Ti(OBu)4 Bogdanović [3] confirms by 57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopyformation of nanoparticles of iron, which upon cycling form an Fe/Al alloy. The fate of Ti in this samples remained unclear, but the indirect evidence supports the formation of a Ti/Al alloy. Furthermore, formation of Ti/Al alloys was observed upon ball milling of the elements [10,11]. By contrast, other studies claim that Ti enters the alanate lattice, and the kinetic enhancement is due to bulk lattice distortions and not to catalytic effects. This is supported by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) [12], XRD [7] and neutron inelastic scattering (NIS) studies, and ab initio calculations [14].

Another drawback of doped-NaAlH4 is the reported loss of stability upon cycling [1,13,15]. The reason for this is poorly understood, but it seems to be due to the incomplete rehydrogenation in the second step (Na3AlH6 to NaAlH4). This in turn might be explained by (a) the reduced availability of Al due to formation of large Al crystallites, possibly accompanied by coating of the Al particles by NaAlH4 [3] and/or (b) delocalisation of Ti catalyst, resulting in its reduced effectiveness [15].

In order to improve the kinetics of the process a better understanding of the mechanism of action of the catalyst during the hydriding/dehydriding process is needed. The present study will attempt to shed some light on the nature and location of Ti-species present after ball milling and during the hydriding/dehydriding reactions.

Regarding the outline of the paper, we mention here for reasons of clarity that Section 3 is structured in three parts, each starting with a presentation of the results, followed by their discussion. These are: (a) the effects of TiCl3 and Ti(OBu)4-doping on the structure; (b) the effects of hydrogen desorption on the structure; (c) cycling experiments at various temperatures. The first two parts refer to the structural changes in the doped alanate system as measured by XRD, while in the last part the kinetic features determined by PCT are correlated with the structural characteristics, allowing identification of the most active catalyst species in the conditions of these experiments.

All preparations were carried out under an argon atmosphere, using a glove box or Schlenk techniques. Ti(OBu)4, TiCl3 and NaAlH4 were obtained from Aldrich Chemical. The TiCl3 99.999% pure was used as received. The as-received NaAlH4 has a nominal technical purity of 90wt.% with Al and Na3AlH6 as the major impurities. The NaAlH4 was further purified from a THF (tetrahydrofuran) solution using the Schlenk technique. Typically, as-received NaAlH4 was dissolved in THF and subsequently decanted from the deposited fraction. The supernatant was then concentrated by vacuum pumping the THF until a precipitate was formed. After drying in vacuum, the purified NaAlH4 was obtained as a white powder.

The Ti-doped NaAlH4 material was prepared by ball milling the alanate together with a prescribed amount (10mol% of TiCl3 or 12mol% of Ti(OBu)4) of the Ti compound. The level of doping for Ti(OBu)4 was chosen such that molar ratio of NaAlH4: Ti was the same as after doping with TiCl3. Weighing of the NaAlH4 and Ti compound was done in a purified argon glove box. The mixtures were then loaded in an O-ring sealed stainless steel ball mill vial under an argon atmosphere. Ball milling was carried out in a high-energy SPEX 8000M ball mill. Three stainless steel balls were used, one of 8g and two of 1g each. The powder-to-ball weight ratio was 1:10. The ball milling time was 1h. To avoid heating and subsequent desorption of hydrogen from the alanate, the outside of the vial was cooled with a flow of nitrogen passed over dry ice over the entire duration of the milling. Thus, the temperature of the bulk was kept under 35°C at all times. X-ray powder diffractograms of the samples were recorded before and after milling.

Dehydriding and hydriding rates and capacities were obtained volumetrically with a carefully calibrated Sieverts’ apparatus purchased from HydroQuebec Company. For temperature dependence experiments (cycling experiments) the temperature of desorption was varied (125, 150, 175, 200, 225°C), whereas the temperature of absorption was kept the same for all the experiments (115°C). The Ti(OBu)4 doped NaAlH4 sample was desorbed only at one temperature, 150°C. The system was evacuated before each desorption cycle and repressurised with 90bar of hydrogen for hydrogen absorption. Heating of the samples for desorption was done at a rate of 1°C/min. The doped NaAlH4 samples were cycled 1.5 times, last step being a full desorption. The same duration for all the de/hydrogenation runs was used in these experiments, irrespective of temperature.

X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded on two different systems, both using Cu Kα radiation, and Bragg–Brentano geometry. One system was a Philips PW 1800 diffractometer with a curved graphite monochromator in the diffracted beam and the other a Panalytical X’Pert Pro system without a monochromator. Transfer of the samples to the instrument was done using special airtight sample holders. The data were collected in the range 2θ =5–85° with a step size of 0.033°. Qualitative analysis of the measured patterns was done by comparison with entries from the ICDD PDF22000 database. When deemed necessary Si was added as a reference compound to the samples to allow for horizontal calibration.

Although much work has been published on TiCl3 doped Na-alanate, the nature of Ti species after doping or during hydrogen cycling has been unclear. The doping reaction was earlier suggested to occur, according to Eq. (1), but no definite confirmation was offered [16]. Although finely dispersed Ti(0) is expected to form in the reduction reaction, previously no peaks corresponding to titanium or a titanium compound could be observed in the XRD pattern. The main crystalline phases detected by XRD were NaAlH4, NaCl, and Al but no information about titanium was obtained. Indirect evidence of reduction to the metallic Ti stage during doping of NaAlH4 with titanium chloride comes from the study of Bellosta et al. [17] in which the volume of hydrogen evolved during the doping reaction, was measured. A ratio of 6 moles H2/mol TiCl3 suggests that reduction of the alanate proceeds up to the zerovalent stage of titanium. These results only confirm the overall stoichiometry of the doping reaction, without referring to the changes occurring post-reduction. It has been suggested, based on the asymmetry of the reflections of aluminium in the XRD pattern for a TiCl3–alanate doped sample, that the finely dispersed Al and Ti metals obtained in the reduction reaction interact with each other to form, e.g. alloys either immediately after doping [8,9] or after hydrogen cycling [3,18].

Although not typical for practical applications, we used very high doping levels (10mol%), in an attempt to facilitate the detection of Ti-containing phases formed upon doping the alanate and in the early stages of hydrogen cycling. Even at these high doping levels, the features seen in the diffraction patterns due to Ti species were always subtle, which made it difficult to monitor their evolution during the H2 cycling process.

Upon doping with TiCl3, the XRD pattern (Fig. 1 ) confirms formation of NaCl and Al in accordance with reaction Eq. (1′).
(1′) NaAlH4 + xTiCl3 →(1−3x)NaAlH4 + xTi+3xNaCl+3xAl+6xH2

Quantitative analysis of the powder data performed by Rietveld refinements further supports the stoichiometry of the reaction. Fig. 2 shows the fit to a sample with 10mol% TiCl3 doping, and 10wt.% Si added as an internal standard for calibration of the horizontal axis. The results of this refinement were: 12±1wt.% Al, 21±2wt.% NaCl, 56±6wt.% NaAlH4, and 11±1wt.% Si. This corresponds well with what would be expected from Eq. (1′) with x =0.1: 11wt.% Al, 25wt.% NaCl, 54wt.% NaAlH4, and 10wt.% Si.

As previously reported [19] part of the alanate might desorb to the hexahydride stage during the ball milling process, as proved by the existence of the hexahydride peaks in the XRD pattern. We found that this thermal decomposition of the alanate can be prevented by cooling the vial during ball milling, as described in the experimental part. In subsequent experiments all ball milling procedures were thus performed with cooling. Consequently, virtually no hexahydride was formed during the doping reaction as evidenced by the near absence of its characteristic reflections in the XRD patterns of the freshly doped material (Fig. 1).

Earlier work by Bogdanovic et al., confirmed that doping of NaAlH4 with Ti(OBu)4 proceeds, according to Eq. (2). Evidence came from the infrared spectra of the reaction products coupled with the measurement of hydrogen evolution during doping [3,17]:
(2′) NaAlH4 + xTi(OBu)4 →(1− x)NaAlH4 + xTi+ xNaAl(OBu)4 +2xH2

The XRD pattern of the post-doping reaction products only contains peaks that can be attributed to NaAlH4, besides the Si reference compound (Fig. 4 ). According to reaction Eq. (2′), there should be no release of Al, which is confirmed by the XRD patterns. Although Ti(0) is expected to have formed, there are no sharp reflections corresponding to Ti or Ti-containing compounds. Moreover, in contrast to the TiCl3 doping, for the Ti butoxide-doped samples, there are not even subtle features that can be attributed to Ti species.

The difference between the two doping reactions might be explained by the formation of free Al in the case of doping with TiCl3 and its absence in the doping with Ti(OBu)4. Following doping with TiCl3, presumably leading to the formation of both free metallic Al and Ti in accordance with Eq. (1′), we observed a feature, albeit subtle, that can be attributed to Ti species, namely a broad reflection ending sharply at 41° 2θ. Oehring et al. [10] reported the development of a similar feature upon ball milling Al and Ti powder blends in a 1:1 ratio. After 105h of milling, the powder pattern revealed a broad diffraction halo, centred just below 40° 2θ and extending to well beyond 42° 2θ. They attributed this feature to an amorphous Al–Ti alloy. Superimposed on the broad diffraction halo were small sharper peaks, the largest one at 41° 2θ. This was assigned to an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution, i.e. normal Ti (0) with some Al substituted for Ti at random positions. Based on the similarity between our observations and those of Oehring we conclude that immediately after doping the alanate with TiCl3 an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution is formed. Formation of supersaturated Ti(Al) or Al(Ti) solid solutions by ball milling elemental powder mixtures of Al x Ti100− x (x =25, 50, 65at.%) was also ascertained in the work of Leonov et al. [20]. For Ti-rich compositions they report formation of an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution. In the case of doping with Ti(OBu)4, there is no free Al available, therefore if any Ti (0) is formed as suggested by Eq. (2′) it probably exists as XRD-amorphous particles.

It has been previously suggested that TiH2 might be the product of doping of NaAlH4 with TiCl3 or Ti(OBu)4 [9]. Interestingly, in the absence of Al in the system achieved by doping NaH with TiCl3, we observed formation of TiH2 as confirmed by the XRD pattern (Fig. 5 ).The reaction is depicted in Eq (3).
(3) NaH+ xTiCl3 →3x NaCl+ xTiH2 +(1−3x)NaH+ x/2H2

Similarly, we expect that if TiH2 had formed in doping of NaAlH4 with TiCl3, the TiH2 peaks would have been visible in the XRD pattern. This is, however, clearly not the case. In both cases, the conditions used are conducive to formation of TiH2. The enthalpy of formation of TiH2 at 298K is −30kcal/mol H2. The H2 pressure developed in the vial during ball milling is around 4bar (theoretically derived from the weights of the reactants used), orders of magnitude higher than the equilibrium pressure of the Ti/TiH2 system at RT. For NaH doping, the molar ratio of H2:Ti products is 1.5. For NaAlH4 doping, the molar ratio H2:Ti is 6, so formation of TiH2 is even more favoured. What is then the reason for formation of TiH2 in doping of NaH but not in the case of NaAlH4? We believe that the difference may be ascribed to the simultaneous presence of Al and Ti in the latter case, which probably combine to an alloy, preventing formation of the Ti hydride. Moreover, there are arguments in the literature showing that formation of Ti–Al alloys is thermodynamically preferred over TiH2 in the presence of Al [9].

Formation of crystalline TiH2 can also be excluded for the case of doping the alanate with Ti(OBu)4, as the XRD patterns reveal no peaks belonging to this compound. Although the system is similar to the TiCl3 doped NaH as regards the absence of formation of Al upon doping and although TiH2 would be an expected reaction product, this is not the case. The absence of TiH2 might be explained by the rapid loss of hydrogen during the doping reaction. It was observed that upon addition to the alanate of Ti(OBu)4, which is a liquid doping agent, the colour of the mixture changes instantaneously (pink) and bubbles are released, pointing to a rapid hydrogen evolution. It is likely that a significant amount of hydrogen is lost in this way, prior to closing the ball-mill vial. A similarly fast hydrogen evolution was observed by Bellosta von Colbe et al. [17] upon adding liquid TiCl4 to NaAlH4. To confirm this hypothesis we plan to perform similar experiments in a closed system where the loss of hydrogen will be avoided.

The propensity of Ti to combine with Al was furthermore demonstrated by an experiment in which the unreacted NaAlH4 was removed from a 10 mol% TiCl3-doped sample by selectively dissolving it in THF. TiCl3-doped NaAlH4 was suspended in THF, stirred, and the mixture left to separate. The supernatant was decanted from the precipitated fraction and vacuum dried. XRD analysis of the dried supernatant fraction showed that it consisted of NaAlH4 (soluble in THF) while the deposit contained only NaCl and Al, demonstrating quantitative, selective separation. Heating the deposit to 475°C induced the appearance of a new peak. (Fig. 6 )

The powder pattern of the supernatant fraction shows no clear evidence for Ti, suggesting that this is most likely present in the precipitated fraction together with the Al and the NaCl phases. The increased content of Ti in the precipitated fraction achieved by removal of the unreacted NaAlH4 permitted observation of a broad reflection at around 2θ ≈41° which we interpreted earlier in the article as indicative of an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution. Confirmation that Ti is not incorporated into the NaAlH4 lattice, but that it is present in the same fraction with Al and NaCl, was obtained from XRD on the precipitate after heating at 475°C. It showed the appearance of a sharp reflection at about 2θ ≈39°, in the region where various Ti–Al intermetallics show reflections. Following thermal treatment, the intensity of the main Al peak is reduced compared to before heating, while the intensity of the peaks from the other compounds remains constant, suggesting consumption of Al. The position of the new reflection, coupled with the relative decrease in intensity of the Al peak, point to formation of a Ti–Al intermetallic, confirming the presence of Ti in the original precipitate.

Formation of a Ti–Al alloy upon heating a system similar to ours was also suggested by Weidenthaler et al. [19]. They doped NaAlH4 with 4mol% TiCl3 and extracted the unreacted alanate components with THF. An XRD of the precipitate after extraction, showed an asymmetry on the high angle side of the main Al peak, similar to our observations. Heating the solid to 830°C showed that it was transformed in Al3Ti. At intermediate temperatures this transition gradually developed.

The results from the above experiments confirm the increased tendency of Ti to combine with Al. After doping NaAlH4 with TiCl3, it is possible that the hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution and amorphous Ti coexist. In the absence of Al, we observed formation of TiH2, according to the equilibrium reaction (4) whereas doping NaAlH4 leads to the formation of Ti–Al species, which ultimately form an intermetallic after exposure to too high temperatures. This alloying would result in a shift of the equilibrium to the Ti-side, which would explain the absence of TiH2:
(4)
Ti
H 2
⇄
H 2
Ti
⟶
Al
Ti / Al
alloy

Figs. 7 and 8
present the changes occurring upon desorption of hydrogen from TiCl3 and Ti(OBu)4-doped NaAlH4. The diffractograms confirm the two steps of the hydrogen desorption reaction, with the main crystalline phases detected being Al, NaCl and also Na3AlH6 in the first, and NaH in the second dehydrogenation step, respectively, as shown in reactions Eqs. (1) and (2). Our PCT data, similar to those in the published literature, corroborate the diffraction results, confirming that two steps are involved in the hydrogen cycling process.

Initially, we have concentrated our attention to the main Al peak region around 38.5° 2θ, which is the only Al peak free from overlap with peaks from other crystalline products. NaCl, one of the reaction products formed upon doping with TiCl3 has reflections in the same regions as the other Al peaks, hindering observation of the features of interest (Fig. 7). On the other hand, the product obtained when using Ti(OBu)4 as a doping agent contains no NaCl. Consequently, the Al peaks in the powder pattern do not overlap with other peaks. Further complimentary information can therefore be extracted by analysis of these peaks (Fig. 8).

The XRD patterns of the Ti halide-doped samples show for all desorption temperatures similar modifications occurring in the main Al peak region upon desorption of hydrogen at relatively high temperatures, namely formation of an asymmetrical broadening of the main Al peak towards the high-angle side. An increase in the desorption temperature results in further broadening of this peak, such that for samples desorbed at higher temperatures the Al peak is broader than for samples desorbed at lower temperatures. By contrast, the Al peak remains symmetrical in the undoped sample during desorption (Fig. 9 ).

A further increase in the temperature of desorption up to 475°C resulted in the same changes in the XRD pattern as we previously observed upon thermal treatment of the precipitate from THF separation. The asymmetrical broadening of the main Al peak changes now into a separate reflection (Fig. 10 ). Concurrently, the main Al peak becomes narrower and symmetrical, similar in shape and position to the Al reflection in the undoped sample, indicating the presence of metallic Al. Additionally, two new peaks are now observed at 40.1 and 40.6° 2θ and we tentatively assign them to Ti hydrides with different H contents TiHx, x =0< x<2. As suggested earlier in the article we believe that the observed changes are indicative of the formation of an intermetallic compound. The stoichiometry of this intermetallic is uncertain at this moment mainly due to the fact that the other reflections belonging to this compound are masked by reflections from other ones (NaCl). Al x Ti alloys with various compositions (x =1, 2, 3) have the main reflection at similar positions (∼38.5° 2θ) as where the new peak develops [21]. Therefore it is impossible to distinguish between these possibilities based solely on the position of the main peak. Likewise, Leonov et al. [20] reported that upon heating to high temperatures (586–687°C) a mixture of fcc and hcp Al65Ti35 solid solutions obtained by ball milling elemental powders, another phase forms, which has been identified by XRD as an ordered cubic L12 phase.

The next step was to determine the effect of desorption temperature on the nature of the Ti species and its influence on the de/hydrogenation rate. The kinetics of the hydrogen desorption reaction was monitored at various temperatures and the XRD patterns of the samples were recorded after each full desorption cycle. Consequently, a correlation could be established between the state of Ti as derived from the powder patterns, and the catalytic activity of Ti in the respective state. The connection between the Ti-XRD features and the catalytic activity is definite, but interpretation of these features is tentative and more work is planned to further confirm our hypotheses.

We performed kinetic experiments, in which the rate of dehydrogenation for each sample was monitored at various temperatures. At each temperature the samples were cycled 1.5 times, so that they had undergone two desorptions and one reabsorption step, starting with the samples in the fully hydrogenated stage. Fig. 11 shows the dehydrogenation rate curves for the 1st desorption half cycle. The heating profiles applied are described by the dotted lines. The rate curves should be identical as the samples have the same composition. The first step of the 1st desorption half cycle, from NaAlH4 to Na3AlH6, is completed at temperatures lower than 125°C. The curves for each sample are identical up to this temperature. From this point on, the curves start to deviate from each other, as the end temperatures become different.

For the second desorption half cycle the rate of desorption is affected by the history of the sample, i.e. the temperature at which the previous desorption was performed. Fig. 12 shows the rate curves for the second desorption half cycle, but only in the range 0–7000s in which the temperatures applied are still identical. It can be seen that there are two factors at play, which have an opposite effect on the rate of desorption. The first is the cycling, which has the effect of shifting the desorption curves from the second half cycle to shorter times relative to the first one. This might be explained by a better dispersion of the catalyst that occurs during cycling, thus a faster system than the initial, previously undesorbed one. The second is the temperature, which shows that the higher the temperature applied during desorption in the first cycle, the slower the rate of the subsequent desorption in the second cycle, as proved by the shift of the curves to longer times with increasing temperature (Fig. 12).

The rates reflect the catalytic activity, thus a low temperature during desorption results in an increased catalytic activity, while higher temperatures lead to a deactivation. This is also clearly demonstrated by the re-absorption curves (Fig. 13 ), which reveal that the higher the temperature applied during desorption, the lower the reabsorption rate is.

The XRD patterns of the samples after cycling were recorded in the range from 36 to 48°2θ. In order to enable a good comparison between the samples, the vertical scales were normalized. This is necessary, because the samples may have slightly different mass densities. For the renormalization, we assumed that the total integrated intensity between 2θ =37.3° and 43.0°, covering the main Al-peak plus any broadening or tailing to the high-angle side, was the same for all samples. We have also horizontally aligned the patterns using the internal reference Si peak. Figs. 14–16
show detailed views of the area around the main and the second largest Al peak respectively.

The XRD patterns from the samples desorbed at 175°C and below are very similar showing that at these low temperatures the main feature that develops during dehydrogenation is a broad reflection near the main Al peak that extends up to more than 42° 2θ (Fig. 14). A significant difference however is seen for the samples desorbed at higher temperatures (200 and 225°C). The main feature developing here is an asymmetrical broadening and the development of a shoulder in the Al peak towards the high-angle side (Figs. 15 and 16). At the same time, the intensity of the broad reflection seen in the samples desorbed at lower temperature, decreases with increasing temperature. Comparing the XRD patterns at various temperatures it can be seen that the increase in the asymmetrical broadening happens at the expense of the broad reflection extending up to 42° 2θ. At successively higher desorption temperatures these two features evolve in parallel i.e. the low reflection progressively disappears, while the broadening of the Al peak increases (Figs. 14 and 15).

None of the patterns show the steep decrease in intensity at 2θ =41°, as observed directly after doping. It seems that the Ti-compound formed after doping, changes its structure within 1.5 cycles, even when the alanate was desorbed at temperatures as low as 125°C. In addition, for desorption at temperatures of 200 and 225°C, the Ti-compound changes to yet another structure, characterized by the asymmetrical broadening of the main Al peak.

Correlation of the reflections belonging to Ti in the XRD patterns with the rate of reaction show that the presence of the broad reflection is associated with increased rates, while the asymmetrical broadening results in decreased reaction rates. It can be inferred that Ti in the compound that gives rise to the broad reflection extending beyond 42° 2θ corresponds to the most active catalyst species under the conditions of our experiments. Development of the asymmetrical broadening results in a decreased desorption/absorption rate, suggesting that Ti in this state/compound has a lowered catalytic activity. Likewise, after the 1st desorption half cycle, the broad reflection ending sharply at 41° 2θ degrees that was observed in the freshly doped sample disappears. In the following cycles, depending on the temperature used in the previous cycle, different features are developing, namely the broad reflection extending beyond 42° 2θ degrees at lower temperatures (up to 175°C) and the asymmetrical broadening at higher temperatures (200°C and above). A tentative interpretation of the XRD features will be given below.

When the Ti-butoxide doped sample, the desorption of hydrogen from a sample that has already undergone desorption, i.e. in the 2nd desorption half cycle, is faster than desorption from the freshly doped sample (1st desorption half cycle) (Fig. 17 ). This indicates as in the TiCl3 case that a first desorption results in a subsequent increase in the catalytic activity.

Although no peaks corresponding to Ti(0)/Ti-compounds were observed in the freshly doped sample, the XRD pattern after cycling shows appearance of a broad reflection that extends beyond 42° 2θ (Fig. 8). This is very similar to what was observed for the TiCl3 doped material cycled at lower temperatures (125–175°C) (Fig. 14). Furthermore, the broadening is observed for all Al peaks.

Although the doping reactions of NaAlH4 with TiCl3 and Ti(OBu)4 are different and lead to formation of different Ti-species, after cycling both systems show formation of the same, catalytically most active Ti-compound, characterized by the broad reflection that extends to beyond 42° 2θ. This is attributed to the formation of metallic Al after a first desorption in the Ti-butoxide doped system, which would make it similar to the Ti-halide doped case.
Fig. 18 shows that the amount of hydrogen reabsorbed by the Ti(OBu)4-doped sample was considerably lower than the amount initially desorbed: 1.6 versus 2.4wt.%.

Bogdanovic et al. [18] attributes this incomplete reabsorption to the coverage of aluminium particles by NaAlH4 formed in the hydrogen absorption process. This would lower their accessibility and, consequently, their reactivity. It should be noticed that the Al/NaH ratio in desorbed Ti(OBu)4-doped NaAlH4 is always 1, whereas in TiCl3-doped NaAlH4 this ratio is always larger than unity, and depends on the doping level applied:
NaAlH4 + xTi(OBu)4 →(1− x)NaAlH4 + xNaAl(OBu)4 + xTi(0)+2xH2 →(1− x) NaH+(1− x)Al+ xNaAl(OBu)4 + xTi(0)+(2x +1.5(1− x)) H2
NaAlH4 + xTiCl3 →(1−3x)NaAlH4 +3xAl+3xNaCl+6xH2 + xTi(0)→(1−3x) NaH+Al+3x NaCl+(6x +1.5(1−3x)) H2 + xTi(0)

For instance, the Al/NaH ratio is as high as 1/0.7=1.43 for a doping level of 10mol% TiCl3. It is likely that this is the reason for the higher degree of re-absorption observed for the TiCl3 doped samples.

Based on the features in the XRD powder patterns we suggest that after doping and cycling, Ti and Al interact to form various compounds, but the nature of these is not very clear. We can, however, speculate more on this by comparison with other literature. Klassen et al. [11] have studied the phase transformations in the Ti–Al system during ball milling and mechanical alloying. Ball milling elemental Al and Ti powders in an atomic ratio of 1:3 for 100h yielded a compound whose powder pattern the authors ascribed to the L12 fcc-phase of the Al3Ti alloy. The pattern is very similar to that of aluminium, but all peaks are shifted to higher angles, apart form the development of two extremely weak reflections at lower diffraction angles. After ball milling for only 30h, the Bragg peaks were very similar to those in the present work after 1.5 cycles. Klassen et al. attributed the asymmetric broadening of the Al peaks to the onset of the formation of the L12 fcc-phase of the Al3–Ti alloy. Upon ball milling Al and Ti in a 1:1 ratio for 105h, Oehring et al. [10] observed the formation of a broad diffraction halo, centred just below 40° 2θ, and extending to well beyond 42° 2θ. This was assigned to the formation of an amorphous Ti–Al alloy. On top of this broad halo they found sharper reflections, the largest of which, at 41° 2θ, they attributed to an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution, i.e. normal hcp-Ti(0) with some Al substituting Ti at random positions. The similarity between these observations and ours leads us to the following speculations:

Initially, immediately after doping NaAlH4 with TiCl3, we note the development of a broad reflection at the high angle side of the main Al peak, centred at 40° 2θ and ending sharply at 41° 2θ. We suggest that an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution is formed (Fig. 3). After doping with Ti(OBu)4, no peaks corresponding to Ti containing phases were present, suggesting formation of XRD-amorphous Ti particles.

Cycling the sample 1.5 times at lower temperatures (175°C and below) transforms this solid solution into an amorphous Al–Ti alloy, as suggested by the formation of a broad reflection extending up to more than 42° 2θ. This probably implies a further incorporation of Al in the Ti. Formation of the compound responsible for this feature results in an increased catalytic activity.

Cycling the sample at higher temperatures (200°C and above) leads to the formation of an ordered Al3Ti intermetallic compound. An asymmetrical broadening of the main Al peak towards higher angles is observed, and at even higher temperatures this feature transforms into a separate reflection. The catalytic activity decreases simultaneously.

We claim that the most active catalyst in our system is the compound that gives rise to the “broad reflection” in the powder pattern, and tentatively assign it to an amorphous Ti–Al alloy. Formation of an Al3Ti intermetallic compound results in a decrease in the catalytic activity. Based on the results above it can be concluded that in order to preserve the most active form of the Ti catalyst and to maintain the cycling stability, the temperature during de/hydrogenation should be kept under a critical value. The mechanism behind the phase changes in the Ti/Al alloy upon cycling remains unclear. It appears that the amorphous Ti–Al alloy is unstable with respect to temperature, and tends to form an intermetallic compound at increased temperatures. This instability probably leads to intermetallic compound formation upon repeated cycling of the Ti-doped alanate. This would explain the poor cycling stability reported by Sun et al. [22], who applied a desorption temperature of only 160°C, but achieved as many as 100 cycles.

Doping of NaAlH4 with TiCl3 or Ti(OBu)4 resulted in the formation of different Ti-compounds, as verified by XRD. The difference between the products formed in the two doping reactions was attributed to the presence/absence of elemental Al in the system. For TiCl3, formation of metallic Al upon doping results in the formation of an hcp-Ti(Al) solid solution. The XRD feature corresponding to this compound is a broad reflection that extends up to 41° 2θ and then sharply drops, at the higher angle side of the main Al peak at 38.5° 2θ degrees. By contrast, doping with Ti(OBu)4, leads to the formation of Ti(0) in the form of XRD-amorphous particles. In the presence of free aluminium no TiH2 was formed when doping NaAlH4 with TiCl3. In the absence of Al in the system (i.e. with pure NaH) TiH2 is formed though.

Desorption of hydrogen from the doped samples resulted in an asymmetrical broadening of the Al peak towards the high angle side. This feature was not observed for the undoped samples, suggesting that the Ti-compounds are responsible for it. At sufficiently high temperatures, this asymmetrical broadening transformed into a separate reflection, while the Al peak became narrower and symmetrical. These changes are indicative of formation of an Al x Ti-intermetallic compound.

Cycling experiments at various temperatures showed that temperature is an important factor influencing the structure of the Ti-compounds formed and consequently their catalytic activity. The most active catalyst for the subject system is the amorphous Al–Ti alloy characterized by a broad reflection on the high angle side of the main Al peak extending beyond 42° 2θ. This is already observed after 1.5 cycles at low temperatures (below 175°C). A decreased catalytic activity was found for the probably more ordered Al x Ti (likely Al3Ti) intermetallic compound that forms after cycling the samples 1.5 times at higher temperatures (200°C and above). The XRD feature corresponding to this compound is an asymmetrical broadening towards the high angle side of the main Al peak (at 38.5° 2θ).

Formation of the more ordered Al3Ti-like intermetallic compound, deactivates the catalyst, leading to the poor cycling stability reported in literature. It is unknown yet, whether this catalyst deactivation can be controlled properly by keeping the temperature below a critical value. Note that temperatures above 110°C are required to deliver pressures of above 1bar H2 necessary for efficient use of PEM fuel cell systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the EU financial support of this research through the Marie Curie Industry Host Fellowship program under contract number ENK-CT 2001-50028. We would also like to express our thanks to Dr. H. Kuipers for the helpful discussions, and to the ASA team for assistance with the X-ray diffraction measurements.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: C.M. Jensen, D. Sun, S.S. Srinivasan, P.Wang, K. Murphy, Z. Wang, M. Eberhard, A. Naghipour, Annual Progress Report of the US DOE Hydrogen, Fuel Cell & Infrastructure Technologies Program, http://www.eere.energy.gov/hydrogenandfuelcells
D. Sun, S.S. Srinivasan, G. Chen, C. Jensen, Rehydrogenation and cycling studies of dehydrogenated NaAlH4 J. Alloys Comp (2004) in press.
S.S. Srinivasan, H.W. Brinks, B. C. Hauback, D. Sun, C.M. Jensen, J. Alloys. Comp. (2004) in press.
